# Sessions CLI reference

This complete offline reference is generated from the built `sessions` command registry.

## Top-level help

```text
sessions — local session fleet CLI

Usage:
  sessions [global flags]
  sessions [global flags] <command> [args]
  sessions help <command>

With no command, Sessions lists agent sessions and headless lanes. Session ids may be full ids or unique prefixes from `sessions ls`.

Daily workflows:
  new                      create an interactive session
  profiles                 list Claude and Codex login profiles
  run                      run a command in a headless lane
  tags                     view or edit session tags
  worktrees                list or safely clean Sessions-created worktrees
  ls                       list sessions
  list                     list agent sessions and headless lanes
  lanes                    list headless lanes
  send                     send text and Enter to a session
  ask                      send, wait, and print the reply
  wait                     wait for session idle or lane exit
  last                     print recent conversation or lane output
  search                   search normalized session chat history
  usage                    report local Claude and Codex token usage
  status                   show a compact session status card
  kill                     terminate sessions or lanes
  recover                  inspect or reopen recoverable sessions
  recall                   inspect integration recall data
  snap                     print the current terminal buffer
  tail                     print or follow recent terminal lines
  transcript               print the full conversation transcript
  input                    alias for send
  keys                     send a named key to a session
  resize                   resize a session PTY
  verdict                  read or emit an explicit producer verdict
  move                     move a session to another endpoint
  adopt                    bind an existing conversation into Sessions

Models and interactive:
  model                    switch an idle session model
  models                   list the live Codex model catalog
  attach                   attach a raw two-way terminal stream

Admin/operational:
  install                  install and start the development daemon
  uninstall                stop and remove the development daemon
  deploy                   explain the retired Node deploy path
  pair                     pair a device with one QR scan
  devices                  list or revoke paired devices
  lan                      manage same-network access
  notify                   configure session push notifications
  remote                   manage tailnet-only remote access
  token                    print the daemon authentication token
  backup                   configure and run session backups
  doctor                   diagnose daemon and session health
  docs                     print the complete offline CLI reference
  help                     show top-level or command help
  version (--version, -v)  print the CLI version

Global flags (must precede the command):
  --json           machine-friendly output
  --host HOST      sessionsd host (default 127.0.0.1)
  --port PORT      sessionsd port (default 8787)

Run `sessions help <command>` for one command or `sessions docs` for the complete offline reference.
```

## `sessions new`

```text
Usage:
  sessions new [--tool claude|codex|shell] [--profile NAME] [--cwd P] [--name L] [--description PURPOSE] [--tag KEY=VALUE ...] [--worktree [--base REF]] [options] [args...]

create an interactive session

Create a session. --tool selects a built-in Claude, Codex, or shell preset; --cmd supplies a command directly. --profile selects a private Claude or Codex login under the Sessions user state; first use opens the tool's own login flow. --description (alias --desc) records why the session exists. Repeat --tag key=value for product, client, team, cost center, or any user-defined dimension. --worktree creates sessions/<name> from the current branch (or --base REF), records its provenance, and runs the session there. Sessions does not create node_modules symlinks; install dependencies in the worktree when needed. Session controls include --model, --effort, --fast, --on-idle, --wait-ready, and --force.

Examples:
  sessions new --tool claude --profile work --cwd ~/work
  sessions new --tool codex --name parser-fix --worktree
  sessions new --tool codex --name release-fix --worktree --base release
  sessions new --cmd /bin/zsh

Global flags --json, --host, and --port must appear before the command.
```

## `sessions profiles`

```text
Usage:
  sessions profiles

list Claude and Codex login profiles

List profile names, private config paths, active sessions, and last-use times. Sessions never reads or copies credentials and has no profile delete command; remove a profile manually only after reviewing the printed path.

Examples:
  sessions profiles
  sessions --json profiles

Global flags --json, --host, and --port must appear before the command.
```

## `sessions run`

```text
Usage:
  sessions run [--name N] [--description PURPOSE] [--tag KEY=VALUE ...] [--cwd D] [--worktree [--base REF]] [--spec FILE] [--wait [--output]] -- <cmd args...>

run a command in a headless lane

Create a headless lane for the command following the first -- separator. --description (alias --desc) records why the lane exists. --worktree creates an isolated Sessions-owned worktree; it does not symlink node_modules. Every child argument after the separator is passed unchanged. Without --wait, print the lane id and return. --wait blocks for completion and propagates the child exit code; --output prints the captured output tail.

Examples:
  sessions run -- make test
  sessions run --name lint --worktree --wait --output -- npm run lint
  sessions --json run --wait -- sh -c 'exit 3'

Global flags --json, --host, and --port must appear before the command.
```

## `sessions tags`

```text
Usage:
  sessions tags <session> [key=value ...] [--remove key ...] [--clear]

view or edit session tags

With no edits, print a session's tags. key=value adds or replaces a tag, --remove deletes one key, and --clear removes all tags. Tags are durable daemon-owned dimensions used by usage reports and the Sessions dashboard.

Examples:
  sessions tags 0123abcd
  sessions tags 0123abcd product=Sessions client=Acme
  sessions tags 0123abcd --remove client
  sessions --json tags 0123abcd

Global flags --json, --host, and --port must appear before the command.
```

## `sessions worktrees`

```text
Usage:
  sessions worktrees [clean [--dry-run]]

list or safely clean Sessions-created worktrees

List worktrees recorded in the Sessions ledger with dirty, merge, and session state. clean removes only worktrees whose session has exited, whose tree is clean, and whose branch is fully merged into its recorded base; every other worktree is skipped with a reason. --dry-run shows the plan without mutation. There is no force option, and killing a session never cleans its worktree automatically.

Examples:
  sessions worktrees
  sessions --json worktrees
  sessions worktrees clean --dry-run
  sessions worktrees clean

Global flags --json, --host, and --port must appear before the command.
```

## `sessions ls`

```text
Usage:
  sessions ls [--mine | --all] [-a | --include-exited]

list sessions

List agent sessions known to the daemon. --mine follows SESSIONS_OWNER_ID, then the SESSIONS_SESSION_ID descendant subtree, then the daemon OS user. The OS-user fallback is user-wide, not invocation-scoped. Exited sessions are hidden by default; -a and --include-exited include them.

Examples:
  sessions ls
  sessions ls --mine
  sessions --json ls

Global flags --json, --host, and --port must appear before the command.
```

## `sessions list`

```text
Usage:
  sessions list [--mine | --owner ID | --all] [--include-closed]

list agent sessions and headless lanes

List agent sessions and headless lanes together. --mine follows SESSIONS_OWNER_ID, then the SESSIONS_SESSION_ID descendant subtree, then the daemon OS user. The OS-user fallback is user-wide, not invocation-scoped. Closed records are hidden unless --include-closed is supplied.

Examples:
  sessions
  sessions list --mine
  sessions list --mine --include-closed
  sessions list --owner team:mine

Global flags --json, --host, and --port must appear before the command.
```

## `sessions lanes`

```text
Usage:
  sessions lanes [--all | --mine [--owner ID] | --subtree ID] [--direct] [--detach]

list headless lanes

List retained headless lanes. --mine follows SESSIONS_OWNER_ID, then the SESSIONS_SESSION_ID descendant subtree, then the daemon OS user. The OS-user fallback is user-wide, not invocation-scoped. --subtree selects session ancestry; --direct limits ancestry to immediate children.

Examples:
  sessions lanes
  sessions lanes --mine
  sessions lanes --subtree 0123abcd --direct

Global flags --json, --host, and --port must appear before the command.
```

## `sessions send`

```text
Usage:
  sessions send <id> [--timeout D] [--no-wait] [--file PATH] <text...>

send text and Enter to a session

Send a message and Enter. Claude and Codex sessions wait for receipt confirmation by default; --no-wait uses fire-and-forget behavior and --file reads the message body from a UTF-8 file.

Examples:
  sessions send 0123abcd 'Run the focused tests.'
  sessions send 0123abcd --file prompt.md

Global flags --json, --host, and --port must appear before the command.
```

## `sessions ask`

```text
Usage:
  sessions ask <id> [--timeout D] [--idle D] [--wait-timeout D] <text...>

send, wait, and print the reply

Send a confirmed message to a Claude or Codex session, wait for the reply to finish, and print the last assistant message.

Examples:
  sessions ask 0123abcd 'Summarize the failing test.'
  sessions --json ask 0123abcd --wait-timeout 2m 'Report status.'

Global flags --json, --host, and --port must appear before the command.
```

## `sessions wait`

```text
Usage:
  sessions wait <id> [<id>... --any] [--idle D] [--timeout D] [condition]

wait for session idle or lane exit

Wait for a session to become idle or a lane to exit. Lane waits propagate the lane exit code. Conditions include --until commit, --until-file-contains FILE STRING, and --until-idle-stable D.

Examples:
  sessions wait 0123abcd --timeout 2m
  sessions wait lane-a lane-b --any
  sessions wait 0123abcd --until commit --timeout 10m

Global flags --json, --host, and --port must appear before the command.
```

## `sessions last`

```text
Usage:
  sessions last <id> [--role user|assistant] [-n N]

print recent conversation or lane output

For sessions, print recent user and assistant messages from the event log. For completed lanes, print the captured output tail.

Examples:
  sessions last 0123abcd
  sessions last 0123abcd --role assistant -n 1
  sessions --json last 0123abcd

Global flags --json, --host, and --port must appear before the command.
```

## `sessions search`

```text
Usage:
  sessions search <query> [--session ID] [--role user|assistant] [--tool claude|codex|shell] [-n N] [--regex | --ranked] [--json]

search normalized session chat history

Search chat history across every live and persisted session known to the daemon. Matching is a case-insensitive substring by default; --regex uses a Go regular expression. --ranked opts into BM25-ranked, stemmed search with quoted phrases and AND/OR/NOT boolean operators, and cannot be combined with --regex. Filters are evaluated by the daemon, so --host can search a remote Sessions instance.

Examples:
  sessions search 'migration plan'
  sessions search 'failed|timed out' --regex --role assistant
  sessions search '"migration plan" OR rollback' --ranked
  sessions search needle --session 0123abcd --json

Global flags --json, --host, and --port must appear before the command.
```

## `sessions usage`

```text
Usage:
  sessions usage [daily|weekly|monthly|session|tag|provider|model] [--mode auto|calculate|display] [--since YYYY-MM-DD] [--until YYYY-MM-DD] [--provider claude|codex] [--dimension KEY] [--json]

report local Claude and Codex token usage

Incrementally index the local Claude Code and Codex JSONL stores, then report token usage and estimated cost by day, week, month, session, provider, model, or one session-tag dimension. Reasoning tokens are reported separately but remain a subset of output tokens. auto uses a recorded cost when present and otherwise calculates with pinned ccusage pricing semantics; calculate always prices tokens; display shows recorded costs only. No usage data leaves the daemon.

Examples:
  sessions usage
  sessions usage weekly --since 2026-07-01
  sessions usage session --mode calculate
  sessions usage tag --dimension product
  sessions usage model
  sessions --json usage monthly

Global flags --json, --host, and --port must appear before the command.
```

## `sessions status`

```text
Usage:
  sessions status <id>

show a compact session status card

Show session state, tool, working directory, git state, activity timestamps, and the latest explicit verdict when present.

Examples:
  sessions status 0123abcd
  sessions --json status 0123abcd

Global flags --json, --host, and --port must appear before the command.
```

## `sessions kill`

```text
Usage:
  sessions kill <id> [<id>...]

terminate sessions or lanes

Resolve each id or unique prefix and request termination. The command exits nonzero if a requested target cannot be terminated.

Examples:
  sessions kill 0123abcd
  sessions kill 0123abcd 89abcdef

Global flags --json, --host, and --port must appear before the command.
```

## `sessions recover`

```text
Usage:
  sessions recover [--all | --reopen [--force]]

inspect or reopen recoverable sessions

List actionable recovery recipes. --all also shows blocked and unresumable lost records with reasons. --reopen creates replacement sessions for eligible records; --force overrides the live or moved conversation guard.

Examples:
  sessions recover
  sessions recover --all
  sessions recover --reopen
  sessions --json recover --reopen --force

Global flags --json, --host, and --port must appear before the command.
```

## `sessions recall`

```text
Usage:
  sessions recall [<full-session-id> [--raw]]

inspect integration recall data

Show the integration-backed recall view, optionally for one full session id. --raw prints the source payload.

Examples:
  sessions recall
  sessions recall 00000000-0000-4000-8000-000000000001 --raw

Global flags --json, --host, and --port must appear before the command.
```

## `sessions snap`

```text
Usage:
  sessions snap <id> [--raw]

print the current terminal buffer

Print the current terminal snapshot. The default cleans terminal control sequences; --raw preserves the daemon response.

Examples:
  sessions snap 0123abcd
  sessions snap 0123abcd --raw

Global flags --json, --host, and --port must appear before the command.
```

## `sessions tail`

```text
Usage:
  sessions tail <id> [-f] [-n N]

print or follow recent terminal lines

Print the last N terminal lines, defaulting to 50. -f keeps following new output.

Examples:
  sessions tail 0123abcd
  sessions tail 0123abcd -n 200 -f

Global flags --json, --host, and --port must appear before the command.
```

## `sessions transcript`

```text
Usage:
  sessions transcript <id>

print the full conversation transcript

Print all user and assistant turns decoded from the session event log. Use the global --json flag for structured turns.

Examples:
  sessions transcript 0123abcd
  sessions --json transcript 0123abcd

Global flags --json, --host, and --port must appear before the command.
```

## `sessions input`

```text
Usage:
  sessions input <id> [send options] <text...>

alias for send

Send text and Enter using the same confirmation behavior and options as sessions send.

Examples:
  sessions input 0123abcd 'Continue.'

Global flags --json, --host, and --port must appear before the command.
```

## `sessions keys`

```text
Usage:
  sessions keys <id> <esc|up|down|left|right|^c|^d|enter|tab>

send a named key to a session

Translate a supported key name to terminal bytes and send it to the session.

Examples:
  sessions keys 0123abcd esc
  sessions keys 0123abcd ^c

Global flags --json, --host, and --port must appear before the command.
```

## `sessions resize`

```text
Usage:
  sessions resize <id> <cols> <rows>

resize a session PTY

Resize the terminal associated with a session to the requested columns and rows.

Examples:
  sessions resize 0123abcd 160 48

Global flags --json, --host, and --port must appear before the command.
```

## `sessions verdict`

```text
Usage:
  sessions verdict <id> | verdict emit <id> [JSON]

read or emit an explicit producer verdict

Print the latest verdict for a session or lane. verdict emit appends a schemaVersion 1 verdict, reading JSON from the argument or standard input.

Examples:
  sessions verdict 0123abcd
  sessions --json verdict emit 0123abcd '{"schemaVersion":1,"verdict":"pass","findings":[]}'

Global flags --json, --host, and --port must appear before the command.
```

## `sessions move`

```text
Usage:
  sessions move <session> --to <target-endpoint> [--token T] [--dry-run] [--allow-dirty]

move a session to another endpoint

Validate and transfer a session to another Sessions endpoint. --dry-run reports the plan; --allow-dirty permits a dirty working tree.

Examples:
  sessions move 0123abcd --to https://sessions.example
  sessions move 0123abcd --to https://sessions.example --dry-run

Global flags --json, --host, and --port must appear before the command.
```

## `sessions adopt`

```text
Usage:
  sessions adopt <path-or-uuid> [--force]

bind an existing conversation into Sessions

Adopt an existing conversation path or UUID as a Sessions session. --force overrides the live or moved conversation guard.

Examples:
  sessions adopt 00000000-0000-4000-8000-000000000001
  sessions adopt ~/.claude/projects/example/session.jsonl --force

Global flags --json, --host, and --port must appear before the command.
```

## `sessions model`

```text
Usage:
  sessions model <session> <model> [--effort LEVEL]

switch an idle session model

Switch the model, and optionally effort, for an idle supported session.

Examples:
  sessions model 0123abcd sonnet
  sessions model 0123abcd opus --effort high

Global flags --json, --host, and --port must appear before the command.
```

## `sessions models`

```text
Usage:
  sessions models

list the live Codex model catalog

Query the Codex app-server model catalog, including supported efforts and service tiers. Use the global --json flag for the full structured catalog.

Examples:
  sessions models
  sessions --json models

Global flags --json, --host, and --port must appear before the command.
```

## `sessions attach`

```text
Usage:
  sessions attach <id>

attach a raw two-way terminal stream

Attach the local terminal to a session. Press Ctrl+Q to detach without terminating the session.

Examples:
  sessions attach 0123abcd

Global flags --json, --host, and --port must appear before the command.
```

## `sessions install`

```text
Usage:
  sessions install

install and start the development daemon

Register the development sessionsd macOS LaunchAgent and start it.

Examples:
  sessions install

Global flags --json, --host, and --port must appear before the command.
```

## `sessions uninstall`

```text
Usage:
  sessions uninstall

stop and remove the development daemon

Stop and remove the development sessionsd macOS LaunchAgent.

Examples:
  sessions uninstall

Global flags --json, --host, and --port must appear before the command.
```

## `sessions deploy`

```text
Usage:
  sessions deploy

explain the retired Node deploy path

The mutating Node-daemon deploy path is retired. Sessions.app is the macOS release and update vehicle; this command exits without changing files, services, or sessions and points operators to the current release documentation.

Examples:
  sessions deploy

Global flags --json, --host, and --port must appear before the command.
```

## `sessions pair`

```text
Usage:
  sessions pair [--name NAME]

pair a device with one QR scan

Mint a five-minute, single-use pairing ticket and print one QR code for the best verified endpoint: Tailscale remote access first, then same-network LAN access. The claiming device receives its own revocable token; the master daemon token is never embedded in the link.

Examples:
  sessions pair
  sessions pair --name 'Uzair phone'
  sessions --json pair

Global flags --json, --host, and --port must appear before the command.
```

## `sessions devices`

```text
Usage:
  sessions devices [revoke <id-or-prefix>]

list or revoke paired devices

List per-device credentials by id prefix, name, creation time, and last use. Revoke resolves an exact id or unique prefix, reports the matched device, and invalidates its token immediately.

Examples:
  sessions devices
  sessions --json devices
  sessions devices revoke 0123abcd

Global flags --json, --host, and --port must appear before the command.
```

## `sessions lan`

```text
Usage:
  sessions lan <enable|disable|status>

manage same-network access

Enable, disable, or inspect explicit HTTP access from other devices on the same Wi-Fi or Ethernet network. Protected routes still require the daemon token.

Examples:
  sessions lan enable
  sessions lan status
  sessions lan disable

Global flags --json, --host, and --port must appear before the command.
```

## `sessions notify`

```text
Usage:
  sessions notify <status|on|off> [done|waiting|lost]

configure session push notifications

Inspect or toggle encrypted push notifications for structured turn completion, sustained waiting, and unexpectedly lost sessions. Omitting the kind from on or off changes all three kinds; delivery begins only after subscribing in the web UI.

Examples:
  sessions notify status
  sessions notify off waiting
  sessions notify on done
  sessions --json notify status

Global flags --json, --host, and --port must appear before the command.
```

## `sessions remote`

```text
Usage:
  sessions remote <enable|disable|status>

manage tailnet-only remote access

Enable, disable, or inspect the Tailscale Serve HTTPS endpoint used for Sessions remote access.

Examples:
  sessions remote enable
  sessions remote status
  sessions remote disable

Global flags --json, --host, and --port must appear before the command.
```

## `sessions token`

```text
Usage:
  sessions token

print the daemon authentication token

Read and print the local daemon token for use by an authorized Sessions client.

Examples:
  sessions token

Global flags --json, --host, and --port must appear before the command.
```

## `sessions backup`

```text
Usage:
  sessions backup <enable|now|status|decrypt> [options]

configure and run session backups

Enable scheduled backup storage, push a backup immediately, show backup status, or decrypt an encrypted backup. Enable requires --project and accepts --interval and --encrypt.

Examples:
  sessions backup enable --project my-project --interval 15m --encrypt
  sessions backup now
  sessions backup decrypt transcript.jsonl.enc
  sessions --json backup status

Global flags --json, --host, and --port must appear before the command.
```

## `sessions doctor`

```text
Usage:
  sessions doctor

diagnose daemon and session health

Report per-session health, spawn path, QoS state, and sessions which should be recreated.

Examples:
  sessions doctor
  sessions --json doctor

Global flags --json, --host, and --port must appear before the command.
```

## `sessions docs`

```text
Usage:
  sessions docs

print the complete offline CLI reference

Print the complete Sessions CLI reference as Markdown. The output is generated directly from the same command registry as sessions help, needs no daemon or network connection, and can be saved or passed to a coding agent.

Examples:
  sessions docs
  sessions docs > sessions-cli.md

Global flags --json, --host, and --port must appear before the command.
```

## `sessions help`

```text
Usage:
  sessions help [command]

show top-level or command help

Show complete command help, or detailed help for one command. sessions <command> --help is equivalent.

Examples:
  sessions help
  sessions help run
  sessions recover --help

Global flags --json, --host, and --port must appear before the command.
```

## `sessions version`

```text
Usage:
  sessions version

print the CLI version

Print the Sessions CLI version and exit.

Examples:
  sessions version
  sessions --version

Global flags --json, --host, and --port must appear before the command.
```
